neighborhoods.name: Sweet Auburn / Auburn Avenue
primary_groups: African Americans from rural Georgia, Alabama, South Carolina
neighborhoods.character: Auburn Avenue was the commercial and cultural spine of Black Atlanta, stretching east from Peachtree Street through a corridor of Black-owned banks, insurance companies, barbershops, and nightclubs. John Wesley Dobbs called it 'the richest Negro street in the world.' The avenue anchored a self-sufficient Black economy born of segregation — Citizens Trust Bank, Atlanta Life Insurance, the Atlanta Daily World, and dozens of professional offices lined the blocks. The residential streets branching off Auburn held a mix of Victorian houses and shotgun cottages.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} arrived in Atlanta around {year}, Auburn Avenue was the center of Black commercial life — a stretch of blocks where a Black family could bank, insure, worship, and shop without crossing a white-owned threshold.

neighborhoods.name: Old Fourth Ward
neighborhoods.character: Adjacent to Auburn Avenue, the Old Fourth Ward was a dense residential neighborhood of frame houses and shotgun cottages that housed Black working and middle-class families. Martin Luther King Jr. was born here in 1929. The neighborhood sat between the commercial energy of Auburn Avenue and the academic enclave of the Atlanta University Center, creating a community where professors lived alongside laborers.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: The Old Fourth Ward was where Auburn Avenue's prosperity met working-class reality — if {ancestor_name} settled here, {pronoun_subject} lived in the same blocks that produced Martin Luther King Jr., in a neighborhood where shotgun cottages stood alongside the homes of Atlanta's Black professional class.

neighborhoods.name: Cabbagetown
primary_groups: White Appalachian migrants from north Georgia, Tennessee, Carolina mountains
neighborhoods.character: A tight-knit white mill village built around the Fulton Bag and Cotton Mill. Workers lived in company-owned shotgun houses on narrow streets, paying rent deducted from wages. The community was insular and fiercely independent, populated by mountain families who had migrated to the city for mill work. The neighborhood's name likely derived from the kitchen gardens residents tended in small yards.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} came from the Georgia or Carolina mountains to work in the mills, Cabbagetown was likely home — a company village where rent came out of the paycheck, the mill whistle set the clock, and neighbors were probably kin.

neighborhoods.name: West End
primary_groups: Initially white upper-class, transitioning to African American middle class by 1950s
neighborhoods.character: One of Atlanta's earliest streetcar suburbs, West End began as an affluent white residential district with Victorian homes and tree-lined streets. By the 1950s and 1960s, the neighborhood transitioned to a Black middle-class community as white families moved to the suburbs. Proximity to the Atlanta University Center made it attractive to Black professionals and academics.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: By the time {ancestor_name} reached West End in the {decade}s, the neighborhood was becoming a center of Black middle-class life — the Victorian houses and wide streets had been built for white cotton money, but the families moving in were building something the architects never imagined.

neighborhoods.name: Peachtree Street Corridor / Downtown
neighborhoods.character: Peachtree Street was Atlanta's primary commercial axis, running north from the railroad terminus that gave the city its original name (Terminus, then Marthasville). Hotels, department stores, office buildings, and theaters lined the street. The corridor was the seat of white economic power in the New South — cotton factors, railroad executives, and Coca-Cola money concentrated here.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} worked in downtown Atlanta, {pronoun_subject} walked Peachtree Street past the marble facades of New South wealth — though the color of {pronoun_possessive} skin determined which doors were open.

major_employers.name: Southern Railway Shops / Atlanta Terminal Complex
major_employers.wages: $1.50-2.50/day (1900s), rising to $4-6/day (1940s) with skilled trades earning more. Black workers confined to laborer grades until WWII-era FEPC pressure.
major_employers.working_conditions: Atlanta was born as a railroad junction, and the Southern Railway shops were the city's largest industrial employer for decades. The sprawling repair and maintenance facilities employed thousands. Work was segregated — skilled trades reserved for white workers, with Black laborers handling the heaviest and most dangerous tasks. The railroad also employed Black firemen and porters.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Atlanta was a railroad city before it was anything else — if {ancestor_name} arrived looking for industrial work, the Southern Railway shops were likely the first employer, where the screech of metal on metal and the smell of hot grease meant a steady paycheck.

major_employers.name: Coca-Cola Company
major_employers.wages: Factory workers earned competitive local wages; office positions paid $50-100/month (1920s-1930s), rising through the mid-century
major_employers.working_conditions: Asa Candler's Coca-Cola grew from a local syrup business to Atlanta's signature corporation. The bottling plants and syrup facilities employed a diverse workforce, though management and office positions were reserved for whites. The company's growth fueled Atlanta's economy and its self-image as the capital of the New South.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Coca-Cola was Atlanta's homegrown giant — if {ancestor_name} worked at the bottling plant or the Candler Building, {pronoun_subject} was part of the company that turned a pharmacist's syrup into the most recognized brand on earth.

major_employers.name: Atlanta University Center (Morehouse, Spelman, Clark, Morris Brown, Atlanta University)
major_employers.wages: Faculty salaries at Black colleges were significantly lower than white institutions: $1,200-2,500/year (1920s-1930s). Support staff earned $300-800/year.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Atlanta University Center was the largest consortium of historically Black colleges in the nation, founded in the years immediately following the Civil War by the American Missionary Association and Black Baptist conventions. The institutions trained the Black professional class of the South. W.E.B. Du Bois taught at Atlanta University from 1897 to 1910, producing the landmark Atlanta University Studies of Black American life.
major_employers.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} had education or ambition, the Atlanta University Center was the reason to come — Morehouse, Spelman, Clark, and Atlanta University were training the ministers, teachers, doctors, and lawyers who would lead the Black South for the next century.

major_employers.name: Cotton Warehouses and Compress Companies
major_employers.wages: $1.00-2.00/day for laborers (1900s), piece-rate work common during cotton season
major_employers.working_conditions: Atlanta served as a major inland cotton market, with warehouses and compress companies concentrated near the railroad terminals. The work was seasonal, peaking in fall and winter. Labor was overwhelmingly Black. Conditions involved heavy lifting, cotton dust exposure, and long hours during peak season.
major_employers.narrative_hook: During cotton season, the warehouses near the rail yards offered {ancestor_name} work that was hard and dusty — baling and pressing cotton was plantation labor moved to the city, but the paycheck came weekly and nobody owned the worker.

port_of_entry_details.facility: Atlanta was an inland destination. Most arrivals came overland — Black migrants from rural Georgia, Alabama, and the Carolinas arrived by rail or wagon. Atlanta's railroad hub status (seven major lines converging) made it the primary destination for rural-to-urban migration across the Deep South. A smaller number of European immigrants arrived via the Port of Savannah or Charleston and transferred by rail.
port_of_entry_details.processing: No immigrant processing facility. Atlanta was primarily a destination for internal migration rather than international immigration. City directories, county tax records, and church rolls serve as arrival documentation.
records_generated: Fulton County and DeKalb County tax digests
Church membership rolls (particularly Black Baptist and AME churches)

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Boarding houses near railroad yards and factories for workers. Black migrants from rural areas often stayed with relatives or church contacts. Shotgun houses — narrow, one-room-wide frame structures — were the dominant housing form for working-class Black and white Atlantans alike.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Racial segregation rather than ethnic clustering defined Atlanta's geography. Black residents concentrated east of Peachtree (Auburn Avenue, Old Fourth Ward, West End). White workers lived in mill villages (Cabbagetown, Whittier Mills, Expo Cotton Mills) or streetcar suburbs. There was minimal European ethnic neighborhood formation compared to Northern cities.
housing_patterns.trajectory: Shotgun house rental (first years) to frame cottage ownership (5-15 years) for Black families who could acquire property. White mill workers lived in company housing for decades. The Black middle class built substantial homes along the Atlanta University Center corridor. Segregation limited Black residential mobility until the 1960s.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Atlanta's primary social division was racial rather than ethnic. The city was a center of white supremacist violence and institutionalized segregation. Racial terror coexisted with the New South booster rhetoric of progress.
specific_incidents: 1906 Atlanta Race Riot — white mobs attacked Black residents for four days, killing 25-40 people and destroying Black businesses on Auburn Avenue and nearby streets
1913 Leo Frank lynching — Jewish factory manager convicted of murder on questionable evidence, then kidnapped from prison and lynched by a mob, reviving the KKK
1915 KKK refounded at Stone Mountain outside Atlanta
nativist_organizations: Ku Klux Klan (national headquarters in Atlanta, 1915-1940s)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Ebenezer Baptist Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community, Auburn Avenue district
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism and marriage records. Church of Martin Luther King Sr. and Jr. Records held by the church and the King Center.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Ebenezer Baptist was the spiritual center of Auburn Avenue — if the {surname} family worshipped there, the membership rolls place them in the same congregation that produced the King family and the leadership of the civil rights movement.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Big Bethel AME Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.community_served: African American community, one of Atlanta's oldest Black churches
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership rolls, baptism, marriage, and death records from the antebellum period. Records held by the church.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Big Bethel AME predates the Civil War — if {ancestor_name} joined the AME tradition, Big Bethel's records may trace the {surname} family from slavery through freedom in one of the South's oldest Black congregations.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Atlanta Mutual Aid Association / Neighborhood Union
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership records, service case files, and annual reports. Founded by Lugenia Burns Hope (wife of Morehouse president John Hope). Records held at Atlanta University Center Robert W. Woodruff Library.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Neighborhood Union organized Black Atlanta block by block — if {ancestor_name} needed help finding work, medical care, or schooling for children, the Union's case files may document the assistance the {surname} family received.

source_references.author: Tomiko Brown-Nagin
source_references.title: Courage to Dissent: Atlanta and the Long History of the Civil Rights Movement
source_references.notes: Comprehensive study of Atlanta's civil rights history from the 1940s through the 1970s, documenting the city's Black institutional infrastructure and its role in the national movement.

source_references.author: Gary M. Pomerantz
source_references.title: Where Peachtree Meets Sweet Auburn: The Saga of Two Families and the Making of Atlanta
source_references.notes: Dual narrative of white and Black Atlanta families, tracing the parallel development of Peachtree Street and Auburn Avenue as the two axes of Atlanta's power structure.

source_references.author: Ronald H. Bayor
source_references.title: Race and the Shaping of Twentieth-Century Atlanta
source_references.notes: Documents how racial politics shaped Atlanta's physical development, including highway routing, neighborhood boundaries, and public services from the 1920s through desegregation.

source_references.author: Clifford M. Kuhn, Harlon E. Joye, and E. Bernard West
source_references.title: Living Atlanta: An Oral History of the City, 1914-1948
source_references.notes: Oral history collection documenting working-class life in Atlanta, including mill village residents, Auburn Avenue community members, and railroad workers.
